target.title: Announce tool plans in chat replies
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds Codex-runtime developer guidance so chat turns that will need tools first prepare a one- or two-sentence, chat-ready acknowledgement that is specific to the user's actual request and workspace voice.
- Sends that acknowledgement from the Codex projector before the corresponding plan progress event in automatic source-delivery turns, so the first visible chat message is the specific human acknowledgement.
- Preserves `sourceReplyDeliveryMode: "message_tool_only"`: automatic projector delivery is suppressed there, and Codex is instructed to use the `message` tool first with the same acknowledgement before continuing.
- Keeps verbose/full plan progress as a separate second message, renders only the numbered plan steps there instead of repeating the acknowledgement, and suppresses repeat plan-list messages when only step statuses change.
- Keeps deterministic redacted fallback copy only for cases where Codex has not produced a usable plan explanation.

## Root Cause

Before this patch, OpenClaw could only build the pre-tool chat reply from deterministic fallback signals such as tool names or plan step labels. That made the first reply accurate about tool usage, but still too generic for real operator asks because the projector ignored the model's plan explanation and the Codex runtime prompt did not require a chat-ready, request-specific acknowledgement.

The first refinement exposed two additional delivery issues. Verbose chat sessions already rendered `explanation + numbered steps`, while the new acknowledgement path rendered the same explanation again at tool start; that produced duplicated start messages and the ordered user experience was backwards because verbose plan progress could arrive before the acknowledgement. Review also caught that automatic `onBlockReply` delivery must not bypass `message_tool_only`, where the message tool owns visible source delivery. A later live Telegram check showed the verbose plan list could still repeat when Codex re-emitted the same plan with only status suffix changes such as pending/in-progress/completed.

Inspected code evidence: `extensions/codex/src/app-server/event-projector.ts` emitted plan events as soon as Codex sent `turn/plan/updated`, and `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.ts` formatted verbose plan progress as the explanation followed by all numbered steps. The patch makes the projector deliver the acknowledgement before emitting the plan event for automatic delivery, keeps verbose plan progress steps-only, gates automatic announcements out of message-tool-only turns, and de-duplicates verbose plan-list progress by comparing plan steps without transient status suffixes.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: a tool-requiring chat request now gets one specific acknowledgement first, only verbose/full sessions get a second plan-list message, and status-only plan changes no longer resend that list.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw temp worktree with Codex app-server projector and shared auto-reply dispatch tests; operator manually verified a live Telegram group-topic run with private identifiers redacted from this PR body; local gateway updated with this PR overlay via `oc-update --skip-codex`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: sent tool-requiring Telegram group-topic requests and inspected the redacted local session records for the affected General-topic and forum-topic turns; reran focused Vitest coverage for the Codex projector, Codex run attempt, Codex thread lifecycle, and shared auto-reply dispatcher; reran test type probe and changed-lanes; pushed head `933e747bd18e270d939e7b6300b4a2c9e45dd4f7`; ran `oc-update --skip-codex` after pushing.

Evidence after fix: the Codex projector tests assert the Beszel/Uptime Kuma-style acknowledgement is sent before the plan event and is not sent again on first tool start; message-tool-only tests assert no automatic source reply is sent and replay metadata remains safe; streamed plan-delta tests assert the fallback announcement refreshes until the first tool starts. The auto-reply dispatcher tests assert verbose plan progress renders only `1. ...` step lines, skips explanation-only plan progress, suppresses repeats when only statuses change, still refreshes genuinely growing streamed plan content, and releases inbound dedupe if an unsent explanation-only plan update is followed by an error. Redacted live-session inspection showed both investigated Telegram turns reached `done` state and had final assistant text recorded; the General topic case follows Telegram's documented `message_thread_id=1` send restriction path.

Observed result after fix: normal mode gets only the concise acknowledgement; verbose/full mode gets the acknowledgement first and a separate numbered plan-list message second; repeated status-only plan updates are suppressed; message-tool-only mode keeps visible delivery owned by the message tool.

What was not tested: no real Beszel or Uptime Kuma credentials/endpoints were exercised; live Telegram proof was operator-observed and redacted rather than attached as a screenshot or recording.

## Verification

- `pnpm exec oxfmt --write --threads=1 extensions/codex/src/app-server/event-projector.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/event-projector.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/event-projector.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose` — 2 files passed, 104 tests passed
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/event-projector.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose` — 4 files passed, 424 tests passed
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose` — 1 file passed, 136 tests passed
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.test.src.json --incremental false --pretty false`
- `git diff --check`

target.title: Recover archived (.reset) session transcripts in memory hook + session-logs skill
target.body:
## Summary

When a session is reset (`/new` or `/reset`), the gateway renames its
`<id>.jsonl` transcript to `<id>.jsonl.reset.<timestamp>Z`. After that
rename, two surfaces silently lose the conversation:

1. **The session-memory hook**, which runs at reset time to summarize the
   previous conversation into the daily memory file. It only ever looked
   at live `.jsonl` paths, so a freshly-reset session was treated as if
   nothing had happened before it.
2. **The `session-logs` skill**, which agents (and users) consult to grep
   historical sessions. Every example used a `*.jsonl` glob and quietly
   skipped both `.reset.*Z` and `.deleted.*Z` archives.

End result: real transcript content sat on disk but was invisible to both
the automated summarizer and to manual searches.

This branch fixes both halves so archives stop being a black hole.

## Changes

### 1. `fix(session-memory): recover archived reset transcripts`

`src/hooks/bundled/session-memory/transcript.ts`

- `getRecentSessionContentWithResetFallback` now scans the sessions
  directory for `<base>.reset.<ts>Z` siblings and reads the newest
  archive when the live transcript is empty/missing.
- `findPreviousSessionFile` gains four ordered fallbacks for archived
  forms:
  1. \`currentSessionFile\` is itself a \`.reset.*Z\` archive
  2. \`<sessionId>.jsonl.reset.*Z\` exists for the requested id
  3. \`<sessionId>-topic-...jsonl.reset.*Z\` topic-variant archives
  4. Any \`.jsonl.reset.*Z\` in the directory (newest wins)
- All existing live-\`.jsonl\` branches run first, so unchanged
  workflows produce identical results.

\`src/hooks/bundled/session-memory/handler.test.ts\` adds three test
cases covering: a \`.reset.*Z\` pointer passed directly, recovery when
the live transcript is empty but an archive exists, and ordering
preference when multiple archives exist.

### 2. \`docs(session-logs): include archived transcripts in skill examples\`

\`skills/session-logs/SKILL.md\`

- Documents the three on-disk filename forms (\`*.jsonl\`,
  \`*.jsonl.reset.*Z\`, \`*.jsonl.deleted.*Z\`) and warns that plain
  globs miss the archived ones.
- Adds a \`list_session_transcripts\` helper and a \`find\` equivalent
  that emit paths from all three forms.
- Updates the \"Search across ALL sessions\" example with both
  active-only and active+archived \`rg -l\` invocations.
- Refreshes the Tips section to mention reset/deleted suffixes
  explicitly.

## Why both in one PR

These changes are two halves of the same bug. The runtime fix lets the
hook recover archives; the doc fix lets the agent know archives exist
and how to grep them. Landing one without the other leaves the other
half silently broken.

## Out of scope (intentionally)

- \`isPrimarySessionTranscriptFileName\` and the doctor's orphan-archive
  scan are unchanged. Their current behavior (treating \`.reset.*Z\` as
  \"not a primary transcript\") is correct for orphan detection: you
  don't want to re-archive already-archived files. Archives become
  *searchable*, not *active*.

## Testing

\`\`\`
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit.config.ts \\
  src/hooks/bundled/session-memory/handler.test.ts
\`\`\`

target.title: media: add shared filename decoder
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: filename decoding is split across media/channel paths, so `Content-Disposition` edge cases and Feishu mojibake fixes are easy to miss or duplicate.
- Why it matters: attachment names with RFC 5987 language tags, non-UTF-8 charset labels, Windows path separators, or UTF-8 bytes misread as Latin-1/Windows-1252 can be saved or delivered with confusing names.
- What changed: added shared media filename decoding helpers, wired remote media fetch through them, and exposed Feishu filename mojibake recovery through the Feishu plugin SDK surface for inbound saves.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not migrate every channel filename path yet, so it is a partial implementation of the broader centralized utility work.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #48788
- Related #48388

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Remote media downloads now preserve more `Content-Disposition` filenames correctly, including RFC 5987 language-tagged values and explicit GB18030, Shift_JIS, and EUC-KR charset labels. Feishu inbound filenames that arrive as UTF-8 mojibake are recovered before saving when possible.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS / local Codex worktree
- Runtime/container: Node 22.16.0 via corepack pnpm
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): media fetch, Feishu filename handling
- Relevant config (redacted): N/A

### Steps

1. Parse RFC 5987 `Content-Disposition` filenames with language tags and explicit charset labels.
2. Fetch remote media with quoted Windows-style header filenames.
3. Recover Feishu-style UTF-8 bytes interpreted as Latin-1/Windows-1252 before saving inbound filenames.

### Expected

- Filename decoding returns readable basenames for supported encoded values.
- Remote media fetch uses the shared decoder.
- Feishu inbound mojibake recovery happens before `saveMediaBuffer` receives the filename.

### Actual

Matches expected in targeted tests.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Targeted verification:

```shell
corepack pnpm exec vitest run --config vitest.unit.config.ts src/media/filename.test.ts src/media/fetch.test.ts
corepack pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/media/filename.ts src/media/filename.test.ts src/media/fetch.ts src/media/fetch.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/feishu.ts extensions/feishu/src/bot.ts
```

`corepack pnpm tsgo` was also run. It still fails on pre-existing repo/toolchain errors unrelated to this change, including missing exports from `@mariozechner/pi-ai` and existing call-signature errors; after fixing one local Feishu compile error, no remaining tsgo error pointed at the touched files.

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: RFC 5987 `filename*` priority, language tags, GB18030 / Shift_JIS / EUC-KR decoding, Windows path basename reduction, Latin-1/Windows-1252 mojibake recovery, remote media fetch integration.
- Edge cases checked: quoted semicolons in filenames, control character stripping, `.`/`..` rejection, normal Latin-1 text fallback.
- What you did **not** verify: live Feishu file receipt against the Feishu API; full migration of Telegram/Discord/Slack filename paths.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert this PR.
- Files/config to restore: `src/media/filename.ts`, `src/media/fetch.ts`, `src/plugin-sdk/feishu.ts`, `extensions/feishu/src/bot.ts`, and related tests.
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: unexpected filename recovery for unusual Latin-1-only filenames, or plugin SDK export bundling issues.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: heuristic mojibake recovery could rewrite a valid Latin-1 filename.
  - Mitigation: recovery only applies when the decoded result is valid UTF-8 and contains non-Latin script characters; normal Latin-1 text is covered by tests.
- Risk: not all channel paths use the new utility yet.
  - Mitigation: PR is scoped as a partial implementation and links #48788 for follow-up migration.

target.title: fix(gateway): prefer physical LAN addresses before bridge interfaces
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #71493.

`gateway.bind=lan` address selection now prefers physical/default-looking LAN interfaces before Docker/bridge/container-style interfaces when advertising automatic LAN URLs.

The change intentionally does **not** hard-ban Docker/bridge/veth/cni/virbr/podman-style interfaces. They are only de-prioritized for automatic selection and remain usable when explicitly configured, or when they are the only candidate.

## Details

- Adds a shared LAN IPv4 picker that prefers:
  - exact common physical names (`en0`, `eth0`)
  - physical/default-looking names (`en*`, `eth*`, `wl*`, `wlan*`, etc.)
  - other non-deprioritized interfaces
  - Docker/bridge-style interfaces as final fallback
- Uses the same selection behavior for pairing setup code LAN URL resolution.
- Mirrors the behavior in the bundled `device-pair` plugin path so plugin-issued pairing codes do not drift from core QR behavior.

## Validation

- `corepack pnpm test src/gateway/net.test.ts src/pairing/setup-code.test.ts`
- `corepack pnpm test extensions/device-pair/index.test.ts`
- `corepack pnpm test src/gateway/server.node-pairing-auto-approve.test.ts`

target.title: fix(gateway): register SIGHUP handler for daemon signal hygiene
target.body:
## Summary

Register a SIGHUP handler in the gateway run-loop as standard daemon hygiene.

A long-running daemon process should never terminate on SIGHUP (terminal hangup). Previously, the gateway had no SIGHUP listener, so Node.js applied the default OS behavior: terminate with exit code 129. Now SIGHUP is logged and ignored, matching standard Unix daemon practice (equivalent to `nohup`).

SIGTERM and SIGINT remain the supported shutdown signals.

> **Note:** This is defensive hardening. The SSH disconnect outage reported in #84249 involves a SIGTERM path from the TUI/systemd lifecycle chain, which is a separate concern. This PR ensures the gateway does not crash on any stray SIGHUP regardless of how it was launched.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `run-loop.ts` | Add one-line SIGHUP handler: log & ignore |
| `run-loop.test.ts` | Two tests: SIGHUP ignored unconditionally; SIGHUP ignored with supervisor markers set |

## Tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.test.ts
 ✓ cli ../../src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.test.ts (30 tests) 75ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  30 passed (30)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Gateway process terminates with exit 129 on SIGHUP because no signal handler is registered. Any process that receives a stray SIGHUP (e.g. from a parent terminal closing) would crash.
- **Real environment tested**: macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`, OpenClaw `2026.5.18 (50a2481)`.

### Step 1: Verify main branch has no SIGHUP handler

```
$ grep -n 'SIGHUP' src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.ts
(no matches — SIGHUP handler is missing on main)
```

### Step 2: Verify fix branch has unconditional SIGHUP ignore

```
$ grep -n 'SIGHUP' src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.ts
127:    process.removeListener("SIGHUP", onSighup);
713:    gatewayLog.info("signal SIGHUP received; ignoring (daemon survives terminal hangup)");
758:  process.on("SIGHUP", onSighup);
```

### Step 3: Real signal delivery — process WITH handler (fix)

```
$ node gateway-sighup-test.mjs &
[PID: 98551]

$ kill -HUP 98551
Process still alive ✓

$ kill -HUP 98551  # send again
Process still alive after second SIGHUP ✓

$ kill -TERM 98551  # clean shutdown
Process exited with code 0 ✓ (graceful shutdown)
```

### Step 4: Contrast — process WITHOUT handler (main branch)

```
$ node gateway-no-handler.mjs &  # no SIGHUP handler
[PID: 98612]

$ kill -HUP 98612
Process terminated with exit code 129 ← CRASH (default SIGHUP action)
```

- **Evidence after fix**: SIGHUP is unconditionally logged and ignored. Process survives multiple SIGHUP signals and only stops on SIGTERM.
- **What was not tested**: The SSH disconnect outage in #84249 involves SIGTERM from the TUI/systemd lifecycle, not raw SIGHUP. That path requires a separate fix targeting the TUI session teardown chain.

target.title: fix(memory-core): skip orphan archival when session store is empty
target.body:
## Summary

When `loadSessionStore()` fails silently and returns an empty object `{}`, the orphan cleanup in `scrubDreamingNarrativeArtifacts()` would archive ALL transcripts belonging to that agent as false orphans.

This bug caused 261 active sessions to be mistakenly archived in a real-world scenario.

## Fix

Added a guard that skips orphan archival for an agent when its session store is empty (`Object.keys(store).length === 0`). Instead of proceeding with a false empty reference set, the cleanup now logs a warning and skips that agent.

```typescript
// Guard: if store is empty, skip cleanup to avoid false orphan archival
if (Object.keys(store).length === 0) {
  logger.warn(
    `[memory-core] dreaming cleanup: session store is empty for agent ${agentEntry.name}; skipping cleanup for this agent.`,
  );
  continue;
}
```

## Root Cause

The original code catches store load failures and `continue`s, but leaves `referencedSessionFiles` as an empty Set. All transcript files then appear unreferenced and get archived after the 5-minute threshold.

## Testing

The fix adds a defensive check at the point where an empty store would otherwise cause silent data loss.

---
Reported by: lemon890918-png
Source: self-investigation and root cause analysis

target.title: fix(session): prefer real tool result over synthetic error in transcript repair
target.body:
## Summary

Fix transcript repair to prefer real tool results over synthetic "missing tool result" errors during deduplication.

When a channel (e.g. Feishu) has a flush-race condition, the session-tool-result-guard may write a synthetic error result before the real result arrives from the channel. On the next session load, `repairToolUseResultPairing` deduplicates by keeping the first-seen result — which is the synthetic error. The user sees "Something went wrong" even though the tool call actually succeeded.

This PR adds `isSyntheticMissingToolResult()` detection and replaces an existing synthetic result with a later real result during deduplication.

> **Note:** This is a transcript-repair mitigation. The underlying flush-race in the session-tool-result-guard (#84134) is a separate root-cause concern tracked independently.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `session-transcript-repair.ts` | Add `isSyntheticMissingToolResult()` detector; track output positions; replace synthetic with real during dedup |
| `session-transcript-repair.test.ts` | 5 regression tests: synthetic-first, real-first, two-real, two-synthetic, span-level replacement |

## Tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/session-transcript-repair.test.ts
 ✓ unit-fast ../../src/agents/session-transcript-repair.test.ts (40 tests) 5ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  40 passed (40)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: When a synthetic "missing tool result" error is persisted before the real result arrives (Feishu channel flush race), the repair pass keeps the synthetic error and the user sees "Something went wrong" even though the tool call succeeded.
- **Real environment tested**: macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`, running the actual `repairToolUseResultPairing` function from the source tree against a realistic Feishu session scenario.

### Failing proof before fix (main branch)

```
$ npx tsx proof-84419-runner.mts  # on main branch

--- Input: session with synthetic-first, real-second tool result ---
  Messages: 4
  Tool results: 2 (1 synthetic error, 1 real success)
  Scenario: Feishu flush race — synthetic error persisted before real result arrived

--- Output: after repair ---
  Messages: 4
  Tool results: 2
  Kept result isError: true
  Kept result text: "[openclaw] missing tool result in session history; ..."

❌ FAIL: Synthetic error was kept
   → User sees "Something went wrong" even though message was delivered
```

### Passing proof after fix (this PR)

```
$ npx tsx proof-84419-runner.mts  # on fix branch

--- Input: session with synthetic-first, real-second tool result ---
  Messages: 4
  Tool results: 2 (1 synthetic error, 1 real success)

--- Output: after repair ---
  Messages: 3
  Tool results: 1
  Kept result isError: false
  Kept result text: "Message sent successfully to Feishu user feishu_user_123"

✅ PASS: Real result preferred over synthetic error
   → User sees successful delivery, not "Something went wrong"

--- Scenario 2: real-first, synthetic-second ---
  Kept result isError: false
✅ PASS: Real result kept (synthetic duplicate dropped)
```

- **Evidence after fix**: The repair function correctly identifies synthetic "missing tool result" markers and replaces them with real results when both exist for the same tool call ID. Both orderings (synthetic-first and real-first) produce the correct outcome.
- **What was not tested**: Live Feishu channel reproduction (requires a configured Feishu bot with the specific flush-race timing). The underlying flush race in `session-tool-result-guard` (#84134) is not addressed by this PR — it mitigates the downstream effect in the transcript repair pass.

target.title: feat(sessions): add adaptive reset mode with daily AND idle semantics
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Session reset currently supports only `daily` and `idle`, and when both are configured the behavior is OR-based, which can reset an active conversation as soon as the daily boundary passes.
- Why it matters: Some users want the daily rollover guard without interrupting active conversations, meaning reset should happen only after the boundary has passed and the session has gone idle.
- What changed: Added `mode: "adaptive"` as a narrow reset mode that requires both the daily boundary and the idle window before a session becomes stale, and updated the schema/help/session wiring to match.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): This PR does not add new reset fields, change the default reset mode, or alter existing `daily` and `idle` semantics.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration

- Why it matters: Existing signals are per-message, sub-turn, or session-level, which makes analytics and post-turn workflows awkward and error-prone.
- What changed: Added a narrow `agent:turn:end` internal hook emitted from `recordTurnCompletion()` on both success and error paths, with targeted tests for the event type guard and manager-level emission.

target.title: feat: add WhatsApp read-only mode
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a true read-only mode for WhatsApp channels so OpenClaw can ingest and analyze inbound WhatsApp messages without ever sending outbound WhatsApp traffic.

Fixes openclaw/openclaw#71166.

## Problem

WhatsApp could emit automatic outbound messages during inbound handling, including access-not-configured notices, pairing prompts, replies, read receipts, reactions, polls, typing indicators, and other transport-level sends.

That made it unsafe to attach WhatsApp in observation-only or production-adjacent setups, because there was no hard configuration-level guarantee that OpenClaw would stay silent.

## Fix

This PR adds `readOnly: true` support for WhatsApp at both channel and per-account config scopes, then enforces it inside the WhatsApp transport as a hard outbound gate.

When enabled, WhatsApp still receives and processes inbound messages, but outbound actions are dropped before they can leave the system.

Covered outbound paths include:

- text and media sends
- polls
- reactions
- typing/composing indicators
- inbound reply/send API closures
- pairing replies
- read receipts
- connect presence

## Testing

- `pnpm tsgo:extensions --pretty false`
- Targeted WhatsApp tests: 4 files, 48 tests

## Notes

This is intentionally enforced at the transport layer so accidental replies from agents, default system notices, or edge-case error handling cannot bypass read-only mode.

target.title: fix(tui): show commentary-phase text when no final_answer blocks present
target.body:
## Summary

- `extractAssistantVisibleText` returned `undefined` when a message had phased text blocks but no `final_answer` block yet, causing TUI to display nothing during streaming and for models that omit `<final>` tags
- Add a commentary-phase fallback between the `final_answer` check and the legacy unphased fallback so streaming deltas and fully-commentary messages are rendered correctly

## Root cause

Commit `22d8e47a50` tightened phase handling: when `hasExplicitPhasedTextBlocks` is `true`, unphased extraction returns `undefined`. This correctly prevents mixing phased and legacy content, but accidentally made all commentary-only windows invisible — the common state during streaming and the only state for models without `<final>` tags.

## Affected issues

Fixes #34513, #35523, #37647, #40824, #34537

## Test plan

- [ ] Send a message to TUI; assistant reply should appear as it streams (before `<final>` tag arrives)
- [ ] Send a message using a model configuration that omits `<final>` tags; reply should be visible in TUI
- [ ] Webchat path unaffected (only commentary-phase fallback added, final_answer still takes priority)
- [ ] `pnpm test src/shared/` green

target.title: fix(compaction): restore session invariants across compaction and reset
target.body:
## Summary

Restore session invariants across compaction and reset instead of patching each symptom in isolation.

This coordinated fix now covers four connected paths:

- subscribe-time `before_compaction` / `after_compaction` hook context
- engine-owned compaction hook context in `compact.queued.ts`
- command `/new` / `/reset` hook context in `emitResetCommandHooks`
- gateway `sessions.reset` hook context in `emitGatewayBeforeResetPluginHook`

It also fixes compaction bookkeeping so zero-token outcomes reset cached usage instead of leaving the session in a repeated safeguard loop.

## What changed

- introduced a shared hook-message-provider resolver so compaction/reset hooks all use the same rules
- normalized explicit providers before emitting hook context, so values like `Telegram` become `telegram`
- inferred providers from session keys only for real deliverable channel-scoped keys
- kept non-channel keys such as `agent:main:direct:peer_123` unresolved instead of emitting fake providers
- reset `totalTokens` / `totalTokensFresh` / `estimatedCostUsd` from compaction results even when `tokensAfter === 0`
- extended regression coverage for mixed-case reset channels, normalized compaction hook providers, zero-token compactions, and gateway/manual compaction bookkeeping

## Why

These reports all describe the same broader failure mode: compaction/reset code paths were not preserving the session invariants downstream systems rely on.

That surfaced as different user-visible bugs:

- hook consumers could see missing or non-canonical `messageProvider` values and split one real conversation into multiple logical sessions
- compaction could clear the transcript but leave stale token/cost accounting behind, causing repeated safeguard loops

## Issues

Fixes #69269
Fixes #69286

Also addresses the duplicate token-accounting report in #69287.
Related cluster: #69202.

## Tests

- `pnpm build`
- `pnpm check`
- `pnpm exec vitest run src/auto-reply/reply/commands-core.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.compaction.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/reply-state.test.ts src/gateway/server.sessions.gateway-server-sessions-a.test.ts`

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: reset/compaction hook provider context now resolves real channel-scoped Feishu session keys, does not invent a provider for non-channel direct keys, and normalizes explicit mixed-case providers.
- **Real environment tested**: local OpenClaw source checkout at commit c59b756105 on Debian 12, Node v25.9.0, using the patched production resolver module via `node --import tsx`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
node --import tsx <<'EOF'
import { resolveHookMessageProvider } from './src/utils/hook-message-provider.ts';
const cases = [
  { label: 'channel scoped Feishu reset/compaction session', sessionKey: 'agent:main:feishu:default:direct:ou_testuser' },
  { label: 'legacy non-channel direct session', sessionKey: 'agent:main:direct:peer_123' },
  { label: 'explicit mixed-case provider from hook caller', sessionKey: 'agent:main:main', provider: 'Telegram' },
];
for (const item of cases) {
  console.log(`${item.label}: ${JSON.stringify({ sessionKey: item.sessionKey, provider: item.provider, resolved: resolveHookMessageProvider(item) })}`);
}
EOF
```

- **Evidence after fix**: terminal output from the patched checkout:

```text
channel scoped Feishu reset/compaction session: {"sessionKey":"agent:main:feishu:default:direct:ou_testuser","resolved":"feishu"}
legacy non-channel direct session: {"sessionKey":"agent:main:direct:peer_123"}
explicit mixed-case provider from hook caller: {"sessionKey":"agent:main:main","provider":"Telegram","resolved":"telegram"}
```

- **Observed result after fix**: live output shows channel-scoped reset/compaction keys carry `messageProvider: "feishu"`, legacy non-channel direct keys leave the provider unset, and explicit `Telegram` is canonicalized to `telegram` before hook context emission.
- **What was not tested**: no browser/mobile UI recording; targeted regression tests cover the gateway reset and compaction RPC paths.

target.title: fix(delivery-context): drop fallback threadId on `to` mismatch and orphan-threadId merges
target.body:
## Summary

Adds two defensive guards to `mergeDeliveryContext` so a stale `threadId` from `fallback` can't leak into the merged result across same-channel cross-conversation shapes the existing `channelsConflict` guard misses:

- **`targetsDiffer`** — both sides have a `to` but they don't match. Fallback's `threadId` belongs to a different conversation, so don't fold it in.
- **`fallbackThreadIdOrphaned`** — fallback has a `threadId` but no `to`. The threadId can't be attributed to any conversation; folding it into a primary that has its own `to` would route the result into an unrelated thread.

A `threadId` is only meaningful inside the conversation it was created in. Without these guards, both shapes silently produce a merged context whose `threadId` came from one conversation and whose `to` belongs to another.

## Scope

This PR is the slimmed-down version of the original `sessions_spawn` thread-routing work in this branch. The primary spawn-site failure mode (#64454) was independently resolved on `main` via gateway-side delivery resolution (`resolveAgentDeliveryPlan` at `src/gateway/server-methods/agent.ts`, route forwarding through `runAgentAttempt` at `src/agents/command/attempt-execution.ts`, and child session seeding in the `agent` handler). Verified working end-to-end on v2026.4.24 (router → `sessions_spawn` worker → reply lands in originating thread).

What remained from the original spawn-site work was a real but adjacent gap in `mergeDeliveryContext` itself — the only safety check was channel-level, so any caller that merged contexts with same-channel different-`to` (or same-channel orphan threadId) could leak. Fixing it at the helper applies the protection uniformly to every caller — gateway seeding (`src/gateway/server-methods/agent.ts`), session-fields normalization (`src/config/sessions/store.ts`), subagent announce delivery (`src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.ts`), and any future merge sites — without touching the spawn flow that's already correct.

## Change

`src/utils/delivery-context.shared.ts`: add `targetsDiffer` and `fallbackThreadIdOrphaned` checks (gated to `!channelsConflict` for `targetsDiffer` so the channel guard takes precedence). When either fires, the merged `threadId` falls back to `normalizedPrimary?.threadId` only — fallback's `threadId` is suppressed. `to` and `accountId` continue to use the existing primary-wins precedence.

## Test plan

- [x] New cases in `src/utils/delivery-context.test.ts`:
  - same-channel `to` mismatch → fallback `threadId` dropped
  - same-channel `to` matches → fallback `threadId` inherited (regression guard)
  - same-channel orphan threadId (fallback has `threadId`, no `to`) → fallback `threadId` dropped
- [x] Existing `mergeDeliveryContext` cases (channels-conflict, channels-match inheritance, fallback-no-channel) all still pass.
- [x] `pnpm tsgo` (core), `pnpm check:changed` (typecheck core + core tests, lint core, import-cycles, conflict markers, webhook + pairing guards) — green.
- [x] `pnpm test src/utils/delivery-context.test.ts` — 14/14 pass.
- [x] Caller smoke tests — `subagent-spawn`, `acp-spawn`, `subagent-announce-delivery`, session store tests — 112/112 pass (no caller regression from the helper change).

## Files

- `src/utils/delivery-context.shared.ts` (+13, -3)
- `src/utils/delivery-context.test.ts` (+28)

target.title: fix: add x-openclaw-session header for stable agent-scoped HTTP sessions
target.body:
## Summary

Partially addresses #68920 (the QMD re-index overhead component).

Every `/v1/chat/completions` call that omits the OpenAI `user` field receives a fresh random-UUID session key. This triggers a full QMD re-index on each request, which accounts for a significant portion of the 10-15s TTFB reported by voice/realtime callers (LiveKit, Twilio, etc.).

Voice stacks cannot always inject the `user` field per-request. This PR adds a new `x-openclaw-session` header that produces a **deterministic, agent-scoped session key** without clobbering the existing full-override behaviour of `x-openclaw-session-key`.

## Priority order (unchanged except for the new entry)

| Priority | Source | Scope |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `x-openclaw-session-key` header | Explicit full override (unchanged) |
| 2 | `user` request field | Agent-scoped (unchanged) |
| 3 | `x-openclaw-session` header (**new**) | Agent-scoped stable hint |
| 4 | Random UUID | Per-call fallback (unchanged) |

## Usage

Voice/realtime callers can now pass a stable call identifier:

```
POST /v1/chat/completions
x-openclaw-session: livekit-room-abc123
```

The gateway resolves this to `{prefix}-session:livekit-room-abc123` scoped to the agent, so warm-session cache hits apply and QMD sync is skipped on subsequent requests in the same call.

## What this does NOT fix

The core 15-20K-token framework system prompt assembly still runs on every request. A `lightContext` / `x-openclaw-light-context` mode that bypasses the full context pipeline is a separate, larger architectural change.

## Test plan

- [ ] Without `x-openclaw-session`: behaviour unchanged — new UUID session per call
- [ ] With `x-openclaw-session: my-id`: same stable session key across calls (verify no QMD re-index on second call)
- [ ] `user` field still takes priority over `x-openclaw-session`
- [ ] `x-openclaw-session-key` still takes highest priority, overriding both
- [ ] Two different agents with the same `x-openclaw-session` value get different session keys (agent scoping confirmed)

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** `/v1/chat/completions` calls without the OpenAI `user` field each received a fresh random UUID session key, triggering a full QMD re-index on every request and inflating TTFB to 10-15s for voice/realtime callers.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw gateway (`openclaw start`), `gateway.http.endpoints.chatCompletions.enabled: true`, macOS 14.7, Node 22.4, auth mode `token`.

**Exact steps or command run after fix:**

```bash
# Call 1 — pass x-openclaw-session hint
curl -sS http://127.0.0.1:18789/v1/chat/completions \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $OPENCLAW_TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -H "x-openclaw-session: livekit-room-abc123" \
  -d '{"model":"openclaw","messages":[{"role":"user","content":"ping"}]}' \
  -w "\nTTFB: %{time_starttransfer}s\n"

# Call 2 — same hint, expect warm-session hit
curl -sS http://127.0.0.1:18789/v1/chat/completions \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $OPENCLAW_TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -H "x-openclaw-session: livekit-room-abc123" \
  -d '{"model":"openclaw","messages":[{"role":"user","content":"ping"}]}' \
  -w "\nTTFB: %{time_starttransfer}s\n"
```

**Evidence after fix:**

Terminal capture — gateway debug log showing session key resolution and TTFB:

```
# --- before patch (no header) ---
[gateway] session key: agent:main:openai:9f4a1b2c-3d5e-4f67-8901-ab2cd3ef4567  (cold)
[gateway] QMD re-index started
TTFB: 11.432s

# --- after patch, call 1 (cold) ---
[gateway] session key: agent:main:openai-session:livekit-room-abc123  (cold)
[gateway] QMD re-index started
TTFB: 10.891s

# --- after patch, call 2 (same header, warm) ---
[gateway] session key: agent:main:openai-session:livekit-room-abc123  (warm hit)
[gateway] QMD sync skipped — session already indexed
TTFB: 0.347s
```

**Observed result after fix:** Calls 1 and 2 with the same `x-openclaw-session: livekit-room-abc123` header resolve to the identical session key `agent:main:openai-session:livekit-room-abc123`. Call 2 skips QMD re-index and returns in ~350ms vs ~11s for the cold call. Without the header the key stays random-UUID and every call is cold.

**What was not tested:** Load behaviour under concurrent voice sessions; LiveKit/Twilio SDK integration beyond curl.

target.title: Fix: apply humanDelay between Telegram streamed block replies (Resolves #68945)
target.body:
Fixes #68945.

The Telegram block-streaming path does not wire `resolveHumanDelayConfig(cfg, agentId)` into `dispatchReplyWithBufferedBlockDispatcher`, so `agents.defaults.humanDelay` / per-agent `humanDelay` overrides have no effect on Telegram block replies.

This change matches the pattern already in Slack, Discord, Signal, iMessage, Matrix, MS Teams, Feishu, Tlon, and Mattermost: import `resolveHumanDelayConfig` through the existing `bot-message-dispatch.agent.runtime.ts` seam and pass its result as `dispatcherOptions.humanDelay` in `bot-message-dispatch.ts`.

## Test plan
- [x] `pnpm test extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts` (74 passed, including new `forwards configured humanDelay to the block dispatcher` case)

target.title: fix(minimax): stop advertising music duration control
target.body:
## Summary

- Stop MiniMax music generation from advertising `durationSeconds` support because the official MiniMax `/v1/music_generation` request contract does not document a duration field.
- Send the MiniMax prompt as authored instead of appending a duration hint that the provider cannot enforce.
- Update MiniMax provider tests and music generation docs so the exposed controls match runtime behavior.

Fixes #84508

## Root Cause

The MiniMax music provider claimed `supportsDuration: true`, but `durationSeconds` was only converted into prompt text. That made the shared `music_generate` capability advertise a runtime control that MiniMax does not actually enforce through a documented structured API field.

## Why This Is Safe

This narrows the advertised MiniMax capability to match the vendor API contract and existing runtime behavior. Callers that request duration through the shared music generation runtime now get the normal unsupported-override handling instead of a misleading MiniMax-specific prompt hint.

Security/runtime controls are unchanged: provider authentication, HTTP request configuration, private-network blocking, response validation, download handling, and mp3-only format validation are untouched.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: MiniMax music generation no longer advertises durationSeconds support because the official MiniMax `/v1/music_generation` API contract does not document a duration request field.

Real environment tested: Local macOS checkout with `MINIMAX_API_KEY` configured from `.env`, using the patched provider code. Secret values were not printed; the live provider log is redacted.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node --import tsx --input-type=module - <<'NODE'
import { buildMinimaxMusicGenerationProvider } from './extensions/minimax/music-generation-provider.ts';
import { resolveMusicGenerationOverrides } from './src/music-generation/normalization.ts';

const provider = buildMinimaxMusicGenerationProvider();
const generateCaps = provider.capabilities.generate;
const resolved = resolveMusicGenerationOverrides({
  provider,
  model: 'music-2.6',
  durationSeconds: 30,
  format: 'mp3',
});

console.log(JSON.stringify({
  provider: provider.id,
  model: 'music-2.6',
  advertisedControls: {
    supportsLyrics: generateCaps?.supportsLyrics === true,
    supportsInstrumental: generateCaps?.supportsInstrumental === true,
    supportsDuration: generateCaps?.supportsDuration === true,
    supportsFormat: generateCaps?.supportsFormat === true,
    supportedFormats: generateCaps?.supportedFormats ?? [],
  },
  requestedOverrides: { durationSeconds: 30, format: 'mp3' },
  sanitizedRequest: {
    durationSeconds: resolved.durationSeconds ?? null,
    format: resolved.format ?? null,
  },
  ignoredOverrides: resolved.ignoredOverrides,
}, null, 2));
NODE

pnpm test extensions/minimax/music-generation-provider.test.ts
OPENCLAW_LIVE_TEST=1 OPENCLAW_LIVE_TEST_QUIET=0 OPENCLAW_LIVE_MUSIC_GENERATION_PROVIDERS=minimax pnpm test:live -- extensions/music-generation-providers.live.test.ts --reporter=verbose
```

Capability and ignored-override evidence after fix:

```json
{
  "provider": "minimax",
  "model": "music-2.6",
  "advertisedControls": {
    "supportsLyrics": true,
    "supportsInstrumental": true,
    "supportsDuration": false,
    "supportsFormat": true,
    "supportedFormats": [
      "mp3"
    ]
  },
  "requestedOverrides": {
    "durationSeconds": 30,
    "format": "mp3"
  },
  "sanitizedRequest": {
    "durationSeconds": null,
    "format": "mp3"
  },
  "ignoredOverrides": [
    {
      "key": "durationSeconds",
      "value": 30
    }
  ]
}
```

Live MiniMax generation evidence after fix:

```text
$ node scripts/test-live.mjs -- extensions/music-generation-providers.live.test.ts --reporter=verbose

 RUN  v4.1.6 /Users/neeravmakwana/Desktop/github_repos/openclaw_repo_prrefresh

[test:live] still running (40s elapsed, 39s since last output)
[test:live] still running (60s elapsed, 20s since last output)
[live:music-generation] attempted=minimax:generate:music-2.6 (env: MINIMAX_API_KEY [redacted]) skipped=none failures=none shellEnv=none
 ✓ extensions/music-generation-providers.live.test.ts > music generation provider live > covers generate plus declared edit paths with shell/profile auth 60367ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  1 passed (1)
   Start at  09:17:15
   Duration  68.90s (transform 6.20s, setup 237ms, import 8.17s, tests 60.37s, environment 0ms)
```

Observed result after fix: MiniMax no longer advertises duration support, the shared runtime normalization drops `durationSeconds` and records it as an ignored override, and MiniMax still generates an audio track successfully through the live provider path.

What was not tested: I did not verify MiniMax duration control because the official MiniMax API reference does not expose a documented duration request parameter.

## Verification

- `pnpm docs:list`
- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm test extensions/minimax/music-generation-provider.test.ts`
- `pnpm check:changed`
- `node --import tsx --input-type=module ...` capability/ignored-override proof shown above
- `OPENCLAW_LIVE_TEST=1 OPENCLAW_LIVE_TEST_QUIET=0 OPENCLAW_LIVE_MUSIC_GENERATION_PROVIDERS=minimax pnpm test:live -- extensions/music-generation-providers.live.test.ts --reporter=verbose`

## Out of Scope

- Adding a MiniMax duration request field if MiniMax later documents one.
- Refactoring MiniMax music generation into async task polling.
- Changing other music providers that already have documented duration controls.

target.title: feat(googlechat): add sessionThread option to bind OpenClaw sessions to Google Chat threads
target.body: AI-assisted: yes (Claude Opus 4.7). ## Summary - Problem: In Google Chat, all messages in a space shared a single OpenClaw session. Starting a new thread to change topic still inherited memory from prior threads, and replies did not reliably land in the user's inbound thread. - Why it matters: Chat threads are the natural topic boundary in Google Chat. Leaking context across threads produces confusing replies ("why does the bot remember something from a different thread?"), forces multiple concurrent threads to queue sequentially on one session, and breaks the mental model users already have from Slack. Requested in #13373. - What changed: Added an opt-in `sessionThread` channel option. When `true`, the OpenClaw session peer id is derived from the inbound thread name, so each Google Chat thread is its own conversation. Replies are now threaded via `thread.name` + `messageReplyOption=REPLY_MESSAGE_FALLBACK_TO_NEW_THREAD`, so bot responses land in the user's thread. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): Default behavior is unchanged (`sessionThread: false` keeps space-scoped sessions). No changes to auth, allowlists, webhook routing, DM flow, reactions, or attachments. No slash commands / `/focus`-style bindings added (see "future work" below). ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes #13373 (per-thread session mode feature request) - Closes #41788 (Google Chat replies creating new threads instead of staying in the existing one) - Related #44347 — resolves the first of the two requests in that issue - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ### Note on naming vs the issue's proposal The issue proposes `threadSessionMode: "per-thread" | "per-space"`. This PR ships the same behavior under a boolean flag `sessionThread: boolean` (default `false` = per-space, `true` = per-thread). Rationale: - A boolean matches the two-state nature of the option and is simpler to document. - The name aligns with the existing session-scoping vocabulary in the codebase. - Happy to rename to `threadSessionMode` with a string enum if maintainers prefer the issue's exact wording, it's a straightforward rename. ## Root Cause (if applicable) - Root cause: The Google Chat channel used the space id as the session peer id and did not pass the inbound thread name back on outbound messages. That coupling meant (a) all threads in a space mapped to one session, and (b) replies could fall back to a new thread instead of the user's. - Missing detection / guardrail: No peer-id unit coverage for Google Chat; Slack already has `prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts` as prior art for this exact pattern, but nothing equivalent existed for Google Chat. - Contributing context (if known): Google Chat threads were less prominent historically in OpenClaw flows; the space-scoped default was acceptable until users started relying on threads to separate topics. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [ ] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: `extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.session-thread.test.ts` - Scenario the test should lock in: `resolveGoogleChatPeerId` returns the thread name when `sessionThread` is enabled and an inbound thread is present; falls back to `spaceId` otherwise; and produces distinct peer ids for distinct threads so sessions do not cross. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The peer id is the single point where "which session does this message belong to?" is decided for Google Chat. Locking its behavior at the helper boundary catches the regression without pulling in the full monitor stack. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): None for Google Chat. Slack's `prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts` covers the analogous primitive. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - New opt-in config: `channels.googlechat.sessionThread` (default `false`). - `false` (default): space-scoped sessions, prior behavior unchanged. - `true`: one OpenClaw session per Google Chat thread; a new thread starts a fresh conversation. - Outbound messages now consistently target the inbound thread via `thread.name` + `messageReplyOption=REPLY_MESSAGE_FALLBACK_TO_NEW_THREAD`, so replies land in the user's thread more reliably. This applies regardless of `sessionThread`. ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text sessionThread: false (default, unchanged) space S, thread A -> session(peer=S) space S, thread B -> session(peer=S) # shared memory sessionThread: true space S, thread A -> session(peer=A) space S, thread B -> session(peer=B) # isolated memory space S, no thread -> session(peer=S) # falls back to space-scoped ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? No - Secrets/tokens handling changed? No - New/changed network calls? No (same Chat API endpoints; only request payload now sets `thread.name` and `messageReplyOption`) - Command/tool execution surface changed? No - Data access scope changed? No (in fact, `sessionThread: true` narrows memory scope per thread) ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS - Runtime/container: Node 22 / local gateway (`pnpm openclaw gateway restart`) - Model/provider: any (provider-agnostic) - Integration/channel (if any): Google Chat - Relevant config (redacted): ```jsonc { "channels": { "googlechat": { "enabled": true, "serviceAccountFile": "/path/to/service-account.json", "sessionThread": true } } } ``` ### Steps 1. In a Google Chat space, mention the bot in thread A and ask an identifying question (e.g. "remember the color red"). 2. Start a new thread B in the same space and ask "what color did I mention?". 3. Reply again inside thread A and ask the same question. ### Expected - `sessionThread: true`: in thread B, the bot has no memory of thread A. In thread A, the bot still remembers "red". Replies land in the thread the user messaged in. - `sessionThread: false` (default): behavior is unchanged from prior releases. ### Actual - Matches expected for both modes. ## Evidence - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after ``` pnpm test extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.session-thread.test.ts ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns spaceId when sessionThread is disabled ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns spaceId when sessionThread is undefined (default) ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns thread name when sessionThread is enabled and inbound has a thread ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId falls back to spaceId when sessionThread is enabled but inbound has no thread ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId produces distinct peer ids for distinct threads so sessions don't cross Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 5 passed (5) ``` - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - When disabled(previous behavior): - When enabled: - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) - Verified scenarios: - Manually verified in a real Google Chat space against a local gateway: - Bot replies land inside the user's inbound thread (both existing threads and thread-starter messages). - With `sessionThread: true`: new thread = fresh session; prior thread keeps its own context; no cross-thread memory leakage. - With `sessionThread: false`: unchanged, space-scoped memory. - Edge cases checked: - Inbound message with no thread (thread-starter): falls back to space-scoped session as designed. - Two concurrent threads in the same space maintain separate sessions. - What you did **not** verify: - Bot-initiated outbound into a space with no prior inbound thread context (no webhook-origin thread). This PR intentionally does not introduce a `threadKey` fallback for that case; the follow-up work below can add richer binding if demand emerges. - DM flow (no behavior change expected; DM scope is already per-channel-peer). ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? Yes - Config/env changes? Yes, additive only: new optional `channels.googlechat.sessionThread` boolean (default `false`). - Migration needed? No. Existing configs continue to behave as before. - If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A. To opt in, set `channels.googlechat.sessionThread: true`. ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: Users relying on cross-thread memory in a space would lose that if they set `sessionThread: true` without realizing the implication. - Mitigation: Default is `false`; docs call out the per-thread isolation explicitly; the name `sessionThread` signals the intent. - Risk: `resolveGoogleChatPeerId` is a new exported helper surface from the plugin's `monitor.ts`. - Mitigation: Kept it narrow (one pure function) and it is only consumed inside the extension itself; no public SDK subpath added. ## Future Work (non-blocking) This PR adds the minimal primitive, matching Slack's auto thread-scoped session behavior, just gated behind an opt-in flag for Google Chat. A follow-up could align with the Telegram/Discord `session.threadBindings` model (`/focus`, `/unfocus`, `/session idle`, `/session max-age`) on top of this primitive. That work requires registering slash commands in the Chat API app config and wiring `SLASH_COMMAND` events, which is out of scope here.

target.title: fix: Remove comments from tsconfig.json to resolve JSON parsing error
target.body:
## Problem
The `qa/convex-credential-broker/convex/tsconfig.json` file contains `/* */` style comments, which cause JSON parsing errors in strict JSON parsers:
```
Expected ',' or '}' after property value in JSON at position 70
```

## Solution
- Removed all comments from the tsconfig.json file
- Kept all compiler options and configuration intact
- TypeScript still supports the config file without comments

## Testing
- Validated JSON syntax with `jq`
- All compiler options remain unchanged

Fixes the JSON parsing error while maintaining TypeScript configuration.

target.title: fix(tts): defer text settlement for final-mode TTS to eliminate churn (#83511)
target.body:
## Summary

When `messages.tts.mode = "final"`, `finalizeAcpTurnOutput()` calls `settleVisibleText()` before TTS synthesis. This flushes the accumulated text to the channel, then the voice note arrives as a separate supplement — causing delete-and-resend churn on Telegram and other surfaces.

This PR defers `settleVisibleText()` until after the TTS outcome is known, and includes the visible text in the TTS payload so Telegram can deliver it as a voice-note caption.

## Root cause

```
Current main flow:
  1. settleVisibleText()     → text message sent to Telegram
  2. maybeApplyTtsToPayload  → voice note synthesized
  3. deliver(voice + supplement) → voice note sent separately
  Result: user sees text, then voice note, then text deleted = churn

Fixed flow:
  1. Skip settleVisibleText()
  2. maybeApplyTtsToPayload  → voice note synthesized
  3. deliver(voice + text as caption) → one combined message
  4. (If TTS fails → settleVisibleText() fallback)
  Result: user sees one voice note with caption = no churn
```

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `dispatch-acp.ts` | Defer `settleVisibleText()` when `ttsMode === "final"` + include text in TTS payload + fallback on failure |
| `dispatch-acp.test.ts` | 4 new tests: deferred settlement for Telegram, eager settlement for non-caption channels, fallback on failure, immediate settlement for non-final |

## Tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts
 ✓ auto-reply (54 tests) 30ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  54 passed (54)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: `settleVisibleText()` called before TTS synthesis in `finalizeAcpTurnOutput()`, causing text-before-audio churn on Telegram.
- **Real environment tested**: Local patched OpenClaw checkout at `6de206309d`, macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`.
- **Failing proof before fix**: Applied PR test file (`dispatch-acp.test.ts` with 54 tests) to unpatched `upstream/main` (`a002c416c7`) source. 1 test fails:

```
 ❯ auto-reply dispatch-acp.test.ts (54 tests | 1 failed) 47ms

 FAIL  auto-reply > tryDispatchAcpReply > does not call settleVisibleText before TTS synthesis when mode is final
 AssertionError: expected true to be false // Object.is equality

 - Expected
 + Received

 - false
 + true

 ❯ dispatch-acp.test.ts:1928:35

 Test Files  1 failed (1)
      Tests  1 failed | 53 passed (54)
```

The failing test proves that without this patch, `settleVisibleText()` is called **before** TTS synthesis (`settleCalledBeforeTts === true`), confirming the churn bug.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:


Fix the Control UI Usage tab default date range.

The Usage view currently initializes with:
- `usageStartDate = today`
- `usageEndDate = today`

target.title: [codex] fix usage channel aggregation insights
target.body:
﻿## Summary
- fix incorrect usage overview aggregation for channels and agents
- fix usage insights so zero-cost channels still surface the correct token-heavy results
- keep `sessions.usage` aggregates scoped to the full matching session set even when `limit` trims the returned session list

## Problem
Usage data could already exist in session files, but the overview still looked wrong in two ways:

- token-heavy channels could appear as effectively `0` or fall out of the visible Top Channels results when the relevant channels had no cost data
- channel totals could be attributed incorrectly at the overview level because `sessions.usage` built aggregates from the limited session subset instead of the full matching session set

In practice this meant a channel such as `feishu` could have non-zero token usage in session records while the usage page still surfaced another channel first or failed to represent the channel totals correctly.

## What This Fix Does
- fixes channel and agent usage aggregation so token usage remains visible in zero-cost ranking cases
- keeps cost-first ranking behavior when cost data exists, instead of broadly changing leaderboard semantics
- updates the usage insight cards so the primary value reflects the token signal users are actually checking in this view
- computes `sessions.usage` aggregates from all matched sessions, while keeping `limit` restricted to the returned session list only
- adds regression coverage for zero-cost channel visibility, cost-first ranking when cost exists, and aggregate correctness when `limit=1`

## Scope
This PR improves token attribution and channel visibility inside the usage dashboard.

It does **not**:
- backfill historical session records that were already written with `usage.totalTokens = 0`
- change provider-side token collection behavior for old runs

## Testing
- `npm exec vitest run --config vitest.unit.config.ts src/shared/usage-aggregates.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/usage.sessions-usage.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

target.title: feat(slack): add contextAware option for slash commands
target.body:
## Summary

When `slashCommand.contextAware` is true, the slash command can be invoked without any text argument. In this mode, it fetches recent channel history (up to 10 messages) and uses that context to respond to the conversation.

## Motivation

This enables a more natural interaction pattern where users can just type `/moss` (or whatever the command is named) and have the agent respond to the recent conversation without needing to explicitly type a question.

Currently, slash commands require a text argument or return "Message required." This makes the interaction feel less conversational.

## Config Example

```json5
{
  channels: {
    slack: {
      slashCommand: {
        enabled: true,
        name: "moss",
        contextAware: true  // NEW
      }
    }
  }
}
```

## Changes

1. Added `contextAware?: boolean` to `SlackSlashCommandConfig` type
2. Updated zod schema to include the new option  
3. Updated `resolveSlackSlashCommandConfig` to handle the new option
4. Modified the slash command handler to:
   - Skip the "Message required" check when `contextAware` is true
   - Fetch recent channel history (10 messages) when invoked without text
   - Build a context-aware prompt with the recent messages

## Testing

Tested manually - when `contextAware: true` and no text is provided, the agent receives recent channel messages as context and responds appropriately.

target.title: fix(agents): handle FastAPI `detail` error payloads to prevent raw JSON leaking to chat
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #44910. When OpenClaw spawns subagents, error payloads using the FastAPI/Starlette/Codex API `detail` field convention (e.g. `{"detail":"The 'gpt-5.3-codex' model is not supported..."}`) were reaching the user's chat verbatim as raw JSON.

**Root cause:** `isErrorPayloadObject` in `src/agents/pi-embedded-helpers/errors.ts` did not recognize the `{"detail":"..."}` shape, so `parseApiErrorPayload` returned `null`, bypassing all error formatting and suppression logic.

**Changes:**
- `isErrorPayloadObject`: add check for `typeof record.detail === "string"` so FastAPI-style payloads are recognized as error objects
- `parseApiErrorInfo`: fall back to `payload.detail` when `payload.message` is absent, so the message is extracted cleanly
- New unit tests covering both `formatRawAssistantErrorForUi` and `formatAssistantErrorText` for `{"detail":"..."}` payloads

**Before:** `{"detail":"The 'gpt-5.3-codex' model is not supported when using Codex with a ChatGPT account."}` forwarded verbatim to Telegram chat.

**After:** `LLM error: The 'gpt-5.3-codex' model is not supported when using Codex with a ChatGPT account.`

## Test plan

- [x] New unit tests added and passing (`pnpm test src/agents/pi-embedded-helpers.formatassistanterrortext.test.ts` — 23 tests pass)
- [x] `pnpm build` clean (no errors, no `[INEFFECTIVE_DYNAMIC_IMPORT]` warnings)
- [x] `pnpm check` clean

> [!NOTE]
> This PR is AI-assisted. The fix is understood and fully covered by new unit tests.

Out of scope (separate PRs per contributor @dsantoreis's comment on #44910):
- Gating provider model probes behind credential-existence checks
- Silently suppressing errors from unconfigured providers at the routing layer

target.title: fix(subagents): notify user on announce give-up instead of silently dropping result
target.body:
## Problem

When the subagent announce retry budget (`MAX_ANNOUNCE_RETRY_COUNT = 3`) is exhausted, `logAnnounceGiveUp` logs a warning, marks the run done, and the completed result is **permanently discarded with no indication to the user**.

This is Pattern 1 from #44925 — documented across 9+ production incidents (E31, E42, E45, E79, E42 × n...):

```
Subagent completion direct announce failed
Subagent announce give up (retry-limit)
Subagent orphan run pruned ... reason=missing-session-entry
```

The user has no idea the task completed. They wait indefinitely or manually ask "what happened?".

## Fix

Add `notifyAnnounceGiveUp(entry)`, called fire-and-forget from the `retry-limit` branch in `resumeSubagentRun()`.

When `entry.requesterOrigin` carries a deliverable `channel` + `to` address (captured at spawn time, already stored in `SubagentRunRecord`), it calls the gateway `agent` method with `deliver: true` to push the result **directly to the user's channel**, bypassing the session-based announce pipeline that just failed.

The notification surfaces:
- a clear warning that delivery retried and failed
- the task label
- the recovered result text from `entry.frozenResultText` (when available)

## Behaviour

| Scenario | Before | After |
|---|---|---|
| announce retry-limit hit, origin has channel+to | silent drop | best-effort last notify to user |
| announce retry-limit hit, no usable origin | silent drop | silent drop (unchanged) |
| last-resort notify also fails | silent drop | silent drop + audit log entry |

Failure is intentionally swallowed — if the last-resort delivery also fails, the outcome is identical to the previous behaviour but the attempt is auditable.

## Scope

This targets Pattern 1 (silent drop after retry-limit). Pattern 2 (timeout with no auto-restart) and Pattern 3 (file creation claims unverified) are separate issues.

Related: #44925, #44919, #43367

target.title: fix(session): preserve mid-turn messages on gateway restart
target.body:
## Problem

Session data loss occurs when the gateway restarts mid-turn (after user message but before assistant reply). The entire session file gets cleared, losing all conversation history.

**Timeline from user report (2026-03-12)**:
- 22:25:21 — User sends message "还是不对，再试试", AI starts processing
- 22:25:33 — Gateway receives restart request
- 22:25:35 — SIGUSR1 signal, process restarts
- After restart — Entire session file lost (including previously persisted history)

**Evidence**:
- Reset marker file found: `6ec0a2d5-...jsonl.reset.2026-03-13T02-17-23.309Z`
- Old session file last modified Feb 22, 3 weeks of conversations lost

## Root Cause

**File**: `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/session-manager-init.ts:44`

The condition `!hasAssistant` was too broad, matching two different states:

| State | File Content | Original Intent | Actual Behavior |
|-------|--------------|-----------------|-----------------|
| Pre-created file | `[header]` only | ✅ Should reset | Resets |
| Gateway restart mid-turn | `[header, user_msg]` | ❌ Should NOT reset | Also resets! |

**Trigger chain**:
1. User sends message, AI thinking (file has header + user message)
2. Gateway restarts
3. `SessionManager.setSessionFile()` loads file, sets `flushed = true`
4. `prepareSessionManagerForRun` detects `!hasAssistant` → clears file
5. All history lost

## Fix

Changed `!hasAssistant` to `!hasAnyMessage` to only reset header-only files (no messages at all).

**Key changes**:
- Only reset when file is header-only (pre-created file scenario)
- If user message exists but no assistant, that's a gateway restart mid-turn — preserve messages
- Added defensive archival before clearing (best-effort backup)
- Updated comments to explain the logic

## Testing

Added comprehensive unit tests in `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/session-manager-init.test.ts`:

1. ✅ New file scenario: only updates header id/cwd
2. ✅ Header-only file: triggers reset (original pre-created file scenario)
3. ✅ **User message but no assistant**: does NOT reset (critical regression test for the bug)
4. ✅ User + assistant messages: does NOT reset

Also added verification script: `scripts/verify-session-init-fix.sh`

## Verification

```bash
pnpm vitest run src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/session-manager-init.test.ts
pnpm build
```

All tests pass, build succeeds.

- What changed: Hardened the UI chat message normalizer and text/thinking extractors to skip malformed content blocks, and added regression tests for those shapes.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): This PR does not change Telegram failed-media delivery, transcript persistence, or backend history sanitization.

Dashboard chat now ignores malformed historical `content` blocks instead of crashing the entire conversation view.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

target.title: fix(daemon): escape systemd cgroup for update handoff via systemd-run --scope
target.body:
## Summary

On systemd-supervised installs, clicking "Update now" silently fails: the update handoff helper is created but immediately killed by systemd's `KillMode=control-group` when the gateway unit restarts. The helper never executes, the version stays unchanged, and the UI shows "Update skipped: managed-service-handoff-started."

This PR detects systemd supervision and launches the handoff helper via `systemd-run --user --scope` to escape the gateway's cgroup. Non-systemd supervisors (launchd, etc.) continue using the existing detached-child path.

## Root cause

systemd's `KillMode=control-group` (the default) kills all processes in the service's cgroup on restart — including detached child processes. The handoff helper, despite being `unref()`'d and spawned with `detached: true`, stays in the same cgroup and is terminated before it can run `npm install`.

## Fix

```
systemd-run --user --scope --unit openclaw-update-<handoffId> --collect -- node handoff.cjs handoff.json
```

Key design choices:

**`--scope` not `--service`**: `--scope` inherits the caller's environment (profile, state dir, config, PATH, proxy, package manager discovery all preserved). `--service` runs in a clean systemd environment and would lose all service env vars.

**`KillMode=control-group` preserved**: The scoped update helper escapes via `systemd-run --scope` into its own cgroup, so `KillMode=control-group` safely stops the gateway and all its child workers without affecting the handoff. No change to existing shutdown semantics.

**Launch failure detection**: After spawning `systemd-run`, the code awaits its exit code with a 5s timeout. If systemd-run exits non-zero (user session unavailable, D-Bus down, etc.), an error is thrown before reporting "handoff started" — preventing the gateway from exiting 80 and leaving the service stale.

**No secret exposure via argv**: The `env` object is passed via `spawn()` option — identical to the existing non-systemd path. No `--setenv=KEY=*** args are used. Secrets stay in `/proc/PID/environ` (restricted to same UID), not in `/proc/PID/cmdline` (world-readable).

Additional hardening:
- `sanitizeSystemdUnitName()` strips non-`[a-zA-Z0-9:._-]` chars from `handoffId`
- `/tmp/openclaw-update-in-progress` marker file for `ExecStartPre` defense-in-depth guard
- `--collect` flag ensures scope units are garbage-collected after exit

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `update-managed-service-handoff.ts` | Detect `supervisor === "systemd"` → spawn via `systemd-run --user --scope`; await exit code to detect launch failures; add marker file lifecycle; add `sanitizeSystemdUnitName()` |
| `update-managed-service-handoff.test.ts` | 3 new tests: systemd `--scope` spawn path with env assertions, ENOENT detection, non-zero exit detection; enhanced mock with `mockChild()` helper |
| `systemd-unit.ts` | Add `ExecStartPre` marker guard, `RestartPreventExitStatus=80`; keep `KillMode=control-group` with updated comment |
| `systemd-unit.test.ts` | Updated KillMode assertion to verify `control-group` |

## Tests

```
$ npx vitest run --project gateway-methods src/gateway/server-methods/update-managed-service-handoff.test.ts
 ✓ gateway-methods (8 tests) 632ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  8 passed (8)

$ npx vitest run --project unit-fast src/daemon/systemd-unit.test.ts
 ✓ unit-fast (4 tests) 2ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  4 passed (4)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: systemd `KillMode=control-group` kills detached update handoff helper on gateway restart, preventing self-update.
- **Real environment tested**: OrbStack Linux VM (Ubuntu 22.04 Jammy, kernel 6.19.13-orbstack, aarch64) with full user-level systemd and `loginctl enable-linger`. Node.js v24.15.0.

### Live systemd proof: `--scope` escapes cgroup, helper survives service restart

Created a systemd user service with `KillMode=control-group` (matching OpenClaw's generated unit at `src/daemon/systemd-unit.ts:86`):

```ini
[Service]
Type=simple
ExecStart=/tmp/handoff-test/gateway-sim.sh
KillMode=control-group
Restart=no
```

**Test A: WITH `systemd-run --user --scope` (this PR)**

```
=== Gateway running ===
     Active: active (running) since Wed 2026-05-20 20:08:58 CST
   Main PID: 2797 (gateway-sim.sh)
     CGroup: /user.slice/user-1000.slice/user@1000.service/app.slice/openclaw-gateway-test.service

=== Helper started (in separate scope unit, sleeping 8s) ===
Helper PID=2799 started in scope at Wed May 20 08:08:58 PM CST 2026
Helper: sleeping 8s while gateway lives...

=== KILLING GATEWAY (KillMode=control-group) at 20:09:00 ===
Gateway service stopped

=== Immediately after kill ===
Helper still running (no sentinel yet — helper still working)

=== After 10s wait (helper finished at 20:09:06) ===
Helper: still alive after 8s! Gateway should be dead by now
Helper: writing sentinel at Wed May 20 08:09:06 PM CST 2026
Sentinel: update-complete
```

**Result**: Helper survived gateway service kill and completed work ✅

**Test B: WITHOUT `systemd-run --scope` (current main behavior)**

Same setup but using plain `bash &` with `disown` (simulating current detached spawn):

```
=== Helper started (no scope, sleeping 8s) ===
Helper PID=2844 (NO SCOPE) started at Wed May 20 08:09:44 PM CST 2026
Helper: sleeping 8s...

=== KILLING GATEWAY (KillMode=control-group) at 20:09:46 ===

=== After 10s wait ===
Helper log: stopped at "sleeping 8s..." — no further output
Sentinel: FAILED: helper was KILLED by KillMode=control-group!
```

**Result**: Helper was killed when gateway service stopped ❌

### Comparison

| Scenario | Helper survives service stop? | Sentinel written? |
|----------|------------------------------|-------------------|
| `systemd-run --user --scope` (this PR) | ✅ Yes — ran in separate scope unit | ✅ `update-complete` |
| Plain detached child (current main) | ❌ No — killed by `KillMode=control-group` | ❌ Never created |

### Security: env via spawn(), not argv

```typescript
// env passed to spawn() for systemd-run — not in command-line args
child = spawn("systemd-run", [...], { cwd: params.root, env, detached: true, stdio: "ignore" });
```

Test coverage verifies explicitly:
```typescript
expect(options.env).toBeDefined();
expect(args).not.toContain("--setenv");
```

### What was not tested

- Full end-to-end `openclaw update` flow (requires published package version delta and npm registry)
- Live `ExecStartPre` marker file guard (defense-in-depth only, verified by code inspection)
- `--collect` garbage collection timing (non-functional, only prevents stale unit name conflicts)

Closes #84068
